topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I watched Sarah across the cubicle partition as she received the congratulations for the Senior Manager role. Her smile was bright, effortless, the kind that seemed to radiate success. I gripped the edge of my desk, my knuckles turning white against the laminate. It felt like a physical tightening in my chest, a dull, persistent ache that wouldn't let up. I meticulously organized the files for the upcoming project, each click of the mouse sounding overly deliberate, a silent protest against the perceived injustice. *Of course,* I thought, staring at the polished sheen of her new title, *she gets it.* I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the carpet echoing too loudly in the quiet afternoon.

[story 2]

"Congratulations again, Sarah," Mark said, clapping her on the shoulder with genuine warmth. I managed a tight, practiced smile that felt brittle on my face. Internally, a cold knot was forming beneath my sternum. I focused intently on the spreadsheet in front of me, the numbers blurring into meaningless shapes. Every time I glanced up, I caught the subtle shift in the air—the way Mark leaned in when he spoke to her, the easy rapport that seemed to flow between them. My jaw felt clenched, my tongue dry. I picked up my lukewarm coffee, the ceramic warm against my palm, and took a slow, deliberate sip, letting the bitterness settle in my throat. I wondered, with a heavy sigh that escaped my lips, if my own contributions were simply invisible today.

[story 3]

I spent the entire morning re-reading the job description for the promotion. The language felt deliberately crafted, tailored perfectly to Sarah’s demonstrated strengths. I kept tracing the lines of the text with my finger, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. When the announcement came, the silence in the breakroom seemed amplified. I forced myself to look at Sarah, offering a polite, almost too-eager nod. My gaze lingered a fraction too long on the silver pin on her blazer. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran down my spine. I moved to the water cooler, the sound of the running tap feeling deafening. I filled my glass, my hand trembling slightly, and avoided looking at anyone, focusing instead on the condensation dripping down the side of the plastic bottle.
